title: javascript 1
content:
1. JavaScript Files vs Text Files

  • .js file is also a text file, but it contains source code.
  • Only difference: color coding in editor.
  • Main difference: text file can’t be run in software, but .js can.
  • JavaScript used to run only in browser, now can run without index.html using Node.js.

2. Node.js

  • Node.js is an environment that allows JavaScript to run without a browser.
  • It turned JavaScript into a backend language.
  • Code is written in VS Code, not Notepad.

3. Checking Node.js Installation

  • Command: node -v → shows installed version (e.g., v20.15.0).

4. Running a JS File

  • node test.js → executes the file.

5. GitHub

  • Used for version control and hosting.
  • Not just a portfolio maker; used to store and share code.

6. Variables

js
const accountId = 12345;    // value cannot be changed
let accountEmail = "mm@gmail.com"; // can be changed
// Avoid var because of scope issues.

7. Comments

js
// single-line comment
/* multi-line comment */

8. Console Methods

js
console.log("Hello World");       // normal log
console.table([1, 2, 3]);         // table format

9. Undefined

js
let meet;
console.log(meet); // undefined (declared but not assigned)

10. "use strict"

  • Enables strict mode in JavaScript.
  • Catches common coding errors.

11. alert()

  • alert("hello") works only in browser, not in Node.js.

12. Semicolon

  • Optional because of Automatic Semicolon Insertion.
  • But using semicolon is best practice.
  • Example error: console.log(3) console.log(4) → missing semicolon causes error.

13. JavaScript Documentation

  • MDN is developer‑friendly docs.
  • Official spec comes from ECMA / TC39.

14. Data Types (Primitive)

  • string – "hello"
  • number – 3, 3.14
  • bigint – very large integers, e.g., 123456789012345678901234567890n
  • boolean – true / false
  • null – standalone empty value (intentional)
  • undefined – declared but not assigned
  • symbol – unique identifier

15. Number Range

  • Safe range: -(2^53 - 1) to (2^53 - 1)
  • For bigger numbers → use BigInt

16. String Quotes

  • Both single and double quotes are valid.

17. Boolean

  • Only true or false.

18. Null vs Undefined

  • undefined → variable declared but no value assigned.
  • null → intentionally empty value (e.g., when you want to send empty).

19. Symbol

  • Creates a unique identifier.
  • Even if description is same, two symbols are different.
js
let id1 = Symbol("id");
let id2 = Symbol("id");
console.log(id1 === id2); // false

20. typeof Operator

js
typeof "meet" → "string"
typeof 42 → "number"
typeof null → "object"   // (historical bug in JS)

21. Type Conversion (Explicit)

Number conversion

js
let score = "33";
let value = Number(score); // 33

score = "33abc";
value = Number(score); // NaN (type is number but value NaN)

value = Number(null); // 0
value = Number(true); // 1
value = Number(false); // 0

Boolean conversion

js
Boolean(1) → true
Boolean(0) → false
Boolean("") → false
Boolean("meet") → true
Boolean(null) → false

String conversion

js
String(33);      // "33"
String(true);    // "true"
String(null);    // "null"

Truthy values: non‑zero numbers, non‑empty strings, objects, arrays, true
Falsy values: 0, "", null, undefined, NaN, false

22. typeof Examples

js
let stringNumber = "33";
console.log(typeof stringNumber); // "string"

23. Arithmetic Operations

js
let v = 3;
let nv = -v; // -3

Supported: +, -, *, /, %, **

24. String Addition & Type Coercion

js
"1" + 2   → "12"
1 + "2"   → "12"
"1" + 2 + 2 → "122"   // first string, so everything becomes string
1 + 2 + "2" → "32"    // first numbers are added, then string
  • + operator: if any operand is string → concatenation.
  • Other arithmetic (-, *, /) convert strings to numbers:
js
"2" - 2 → 0

25. Explicit Conversion

js
let a = "123";
a = Number(a);   // number
let b = 456;
b = String(b);   // string

26. Special Conversions

js
console.log(+true);   // 1
console.log(+"");     // 0

27. Multiple Assignment

js
let num1, num2, num3;
num1 = num2 = num3 = 2 + 2;
console.log(num1, num2, num3); // 4 4 4

28. Increment & Decrement

js
let gameCounter = 100;
console.log(gameCounter++); // 100 (post‑increment)
console.log(gameCounter);   // 101
console.log(++gameCounter); // 102 (pre‑increment)

End of Part 1
→ Type Proceed for next part (Comparison, Data Types Categories, Stack/Heap, Strings, Numbers, Dates).


1. Comparison Basics

js
console.log(2 > 1);   // true
console.log(2 >= 1);  // true

2. String vs Number Comparison

  • JS converts string to number automatically.

js
console.log("2" > 1);   // true
console.log("02" > 1);  // true

3. Null Comparison

js
console.log(null > 0);   // false
console.log(null == 0);  // false
console.log(null >= 0);  // true

  • Reason: == doesn’t convert null to 0, but comparisons (>=, <=) convert null to 0.

4. Undefined Comparison

js
console.log(undefined == 0);   // false
console.log(undefined > 0);    // false
console.log(undefined < 0);    // false

  • undefined is not converted to a number; it becomes NaN.

5. Strict Equality (===)

  • Checks both value and type. No conversion.

js
console.log("2" === 2);  // false

6. Suggestion on == vs ===

  • Always prefer === and !== to avoid unexpected type conversion.

7. Data Types – Primitive vs Non‑Primitive

  • Primitive (Call by Value): String, Number, Boolean, Null, Undefined, Symbol, BigInt

  • Non‑Primitive / Reference (Call by Reference): Array, Object, Function

8. Primitive Notes

  • 7 types: string, number, boolean, null, undefined, symbol, bigint.

  • Symbol makes a value unique.

  • BigInt used for very large numbers.

9. Reference Type Notes

  • Arrays, objects, functions are reference types.

  • They are stored in memory and accessed by reference.

10. Static vs Dynamic Typing

  • JavaScript is dynamic type language – data types are determined at runtime.

  • TypeScript is static – types are defined at compile time.

11. Number Type Notes

js
const score = 100.3;   // number
const outsidetemp = null;
console.log(typeof outsidetemp); // "object" (bug)

12. Undefined Variable

js
let useremail;          // value is undefined

13. Symbol in JavaScript

js
const id = Symbol('123');
const id2 = Symbol('123');
console.log(id == id2); // false   // always unique

  • Symbol’s datatype is symbol.

14. BigInt

js
const bigNumber = 123456789012345678901234567890n;
console.log(bigNumber);

15. Arrays

js
const heros = ["shaktiman", "Nagraj", "doga"];

  • Arrays are reference type (typeof returns "object").

16. Objects

js
let myobj = {
  name: "hitesh",
  age: 22,
};
console.log(myobj.name);

17. Functions

js
const meet = function() {
  console.log("hello world");
};
console.log(typeof meet); // "function"

18. typeof Operator – Full Table (as in notes)

  • "Hello" → "string"

  • 42 → "number"

  • true → "boolean"

  • null → "object" (historical bug)

  • undefined → "undefined"

  • Symbol("id") → "symbol"

  • 123n → "bigint"

  • { name: "Ali" } → "object"

  • [1,2,3] → "object"

  • function(){} → "function"

  • NaN → "number"

  • new Date() → "object"

  • class A {} → "function"

19. Primitive vs Non‑Primitive – Memory

  • Non‑primitive data types’ typeof is mostly "object", except function returns "function".

20. Stack vs Heap

  • Stack → used for primitive types. A copy of the value is given.

  • Heap → used for non‑primitive types. A reference (original) is given.

Primitive (Stack) Example:

js
let a = 10;
let b = a;   // copy
b = 20;
console.log(a); // 10 (unchanged)

Non‑Primitive (Heap) Example:

js
let user1 = { email: "meet@gmail.com", upi: "user@ybl" };
let user2 = user1;   // same reference
user2.email = "changed@gmail.com";
console.log(user1.email); // "changed@gmail.com"

21. Strings

String concatenation (old way):

js
const name = 'meet';
const repocount = 50;
console.log(name + repocount + " value"); // meet50 value

Template literals (modern way):

js
console.log(`hello my name is ${name} and my repo count is ${repocount}`);

String object wrapper:

js
const gamename = new String("meetgame");
console.log(typeof gamename); // "object"

Accessing characters:

js
console.log(gamename[0]); // "m"
console.log(gamename.__proto__); // shows String.prototype
console.log(gamename.length); // 8

String methods (case‑sensitive):

js
console.log(gamename.toUpperCase()); // "MEETGAME"
console.log(gamename.charAt(2));     // "e"
console.log(gamename.indexOf('t'));  // 3
console.log(gamename.substring(0, 4)); // "meet" (end exclusive)
console.log(gamename.slice(-4));      // "game"

trim, trimStart, trimEnd:

js
const raw = " hitesh ";
console.log(raw.trim());      // "hitesh"
console.log(raw.trimStart()); // "hitesh "
console.log(raw.trimEnd());   // " hitesh"

URL encoding / replace / includes / split:

js
let url = "https://example.com/meet%20khurana";
console.log(url.replace("%20", "-"));   // "https://example.com/meet-khurana"
console.log(url.includes("hitesh"));    // false

let gamename = "meet game khurana";
console.log(gamename.split(" "));       // ["meet","game","khurana"]

22. Numbers

Declaring numbers:

js
const score = 400;               // normal number
const balance = new Number(100); // Number object (rarely used)

Methods:

js
let num = 100;
console.log(num.toString());           // "100"
console.log(num.toString().length);    // 3
console.log(num.toFixed(2));           // "100.00"

let num2 = 23.8966;
console.log(num2.toPrecision(3));      // "23.9"
num2 = 123.8966;
console.log(num2.toPrecision(3));      // "124"

let hundreds = 1000000;
console.log(hundreds.toLocaleString("en-US")); // "1,000,000"
console.log(hundreds.toLocaleString("en-IN")); // "10,00,000"

Number constants:

js
console.log(Number.MAX_VALUE);
console.log(Number.MIN_VALUE);
console.log(Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER); // 9007199254740991
console.log(Number.MIN_SAFE_INTEGER); // -9007199254740991

23. Math Library

js
console.log(Math); // [Math: object]

Common methods:

js
Math.abs(-4);        // 4
Math.round(4.6);     // 5
Math.ceil(4.2);      // 5
Math.floor(4.9);     // 4
Math.sqrt(16);       // 4
Math.pow(2, 3);      // 8
Math.max(3,4,5);     // 5
Math.min(3,4,5);     // 3
Math.random();       // 0 to 1 (excluding 1)

Random number in range:

js
const min = 10;
const max = 20;
console.log(Math.floor(Math.random() * (max - min + 1)) + min);

24. Date

Creating a date:

js
let myDate = new Date();
console.log(myDate);                // ISO string
Sat Mar 21 2026 22:36:33 GMT+0530 (India Standard Time)

console.log(myDate.toString());     // readable
Sat Mar 21 2026 22:36:33 GMT+0530 (India Standard Time)

console.log(myDate.toDateString()); // "Wed Mar 01 2023"
Sat Mar 21 2026

console.log(myDate.toLocaleString()); // "3/1/2023, 12:04:46 PM"
console.log(typeof myDate);         // "object"

Custom date:

js
let myCreatedDate = new Date(2023, 0, 23, 5, 3);
console.log(myCreatedDate.toString()); // "Mon Jan 23 2023 05:03:00 GMT+0000"

Date formats:

js
let myDate2 = new Date("2023-01-14");
console.log(myDate2.toLocaleString()); // "1/14/2023, 12:00:00 AM"

Timestamps:

js
let timestamp = Date.now();                     // milliseconds since Jan 1, 1970
console.log(myDate.getTime());                  // same as Date.now()
console.log(Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000));     // seconds

Extracting parts:

js
let newDate = new Date();
console.log(newDate.getMonth() + 1); // month 1‑12
console.log(newDate.getDay());       // weekday 0‑6
console.log(`${newDate.getDay()} and the time is ${newDate.getHours()}:${newDate.getMinutes()}`);

Locale string customization:

js
console.log(newDate.toLocaleString("en-US", {
  weekday: "long",
  year: "numeric",
  month: "long",
  day: "numeric",
  hour: "2-digit",
  minute: "2-digit",
  timeZone: "Asia/Kolkata"
}));

Arrays

Array Basics

  • const myarr = [0,1,2,3,45] – can have mixed data types.

  • Array is a non‑primitive object.

  • Access by index: arr[0] (zero‑based).

  • Array copy operations give shallow copy (same reference), not deep copy.

Array methods:

js
arr.push(6);        // add at end last me lgadentge
arr.pop();          // remove last 
arr.unshift(0);     // add at start start me lgadenge 
arr.shift();        // remove from start
arr.includes(9);    // check existence → true/false
arr.indexOf(9);     // returns index or -1

join() – array to string:

js
newArr = arr.join();   // "10,20,30,40" by default

slice() vs splice():

  • slice(start, end) – non‑destructive, returns new array, end exclusive.

  • splice(start, count) – destructive, modifies original array.

push() with array:

js
arr1.push(arr2);  // adds whole arr2 as one element → nested array

//nested array mtlb array ke andar array ki trh 
[1,2,[2,3]]

concat() and spread operator:

js
arr1.concat(arr2);     // returns new merged array
arr=[1,2] arr1=[2,3] result = [1,2,3]
arr1.push(...arr2);    // spread adds individual elements


// Take all elements of arr2 and push them individually into arr1

//concat also work the same but issue h concat  ek naya array bandeta h original array me change nhi krta

flat() – flattens nested arrays:

js
another_array.flat(Infinity);

scss

hello meet

let arr = [1, 2, [3, 4]];

let result = arr.flat();
console.log(result); // [1, 2, 3, 4]

by default flat() level1 flat krta h 

let arr = [1, [2, [3, 4]]];
console.log(arr.flat()); // [1, 2, [3, 4]]
console.log(arr.flat(2)); // [1, 2, 3, 4]



Utility methods:

js
Array.isArray("hitesh");               // false
// check krta h jo given value h voh array h ya nhi 
Array.from("hitesh");                  // ["h","i","t","e","s","h"]
// string ya kisi bhi chij ko array me convert krdega
Array.from({ name: "hitesh" });        // []   // need keys/values
Array.from() ko chahiye:
    • iterable (like string, array)

    • ya array-like object (jisme length ho)
❌{ name: "hitesh" } na iterable hai na length property hai👉 Isliye → empty array []
Array.from(Object.keys({ name: "hitesh" })); // ["name"]
Array.of(score1, score2, score3);      // creates array from values

👉 Values se array banata hai

✔️ Example:

Array.of(1, 2, 3); // [1, 2, 3]

🔥 Important Difference (Array.of vs Array)

Array(3);    // [empty × 3]  ⚠️ (length 3, values nahi)
Array.of(3); // [3]          ✅ (actual value)

👉 Yeh interview mein bahut poocha jata hai!

Objects

Object Literal

js
const JsUser = {
  name: "hitesh",
  age: 18,
  location: "Jaipur",
  email: "hitesh@google.com",
  isLoggedIn: true
};
console.log(JsUser.name);          // dot notation
console.log(JsUser["email"]);      // bracket notation (for keys with spaces)

const obj = {

  "full name": "Hitesh"

};

console.log(obj["full name"]); // ✅ works

// console.log(obj.full name); ❌ error

delete JsUser.isLoggedIn;


Symbol as key:

js
const mySym = Symbol("key1");
const JsUser = {
  [mySym]: "mykey1"
};
console.log(JsUser[mySym]); // "mykey1"   // symbol key

Freezing an object:

js
Object.freeze(jsuser);   // no changes allowed (silently ignored)

koi change krna chahoge nhi hoga

Adding methods:

js
JsUser.greeting = function() {
  console.log("hello");
};

jsUser.greeting();

JsUser.greeting2 = function() {
  console.log(`Hello, ${this.name}`);
};

Constructor‑based object

js
const tinderUser = {}; //yeh ek empty h isme abhi kuch nhi h 
// chlo add krte h 

tinderUser.id = "12abc";
tinderUser.name = "meet";
tinderUser.isLoggedIn = false;




console.log(user.fullName.first); // meet
nested object

chekc is empty 
Object.keys(tinderUser).length === 0;

Nested Objects

js
const regularUser = {
  email: "some@gmail.com",
  fullName: {
    userFullName: {
      firstName: "hitesh",
      lastName: "Choudhary"
    }
  }
};
console.log(regularUser.fullName.userFullName.firstName);

Merging Objects

  • const obj3 = { obj1, obj2 } → nested, not merged.

  • Object.assign({}, obj1, obj2) → merges into new object.

  • yeh jo khali diya h isme ob1 or 2 daaldega

  • agar {} nhi lgayenge toh ob1 modify hojayega 

  • Spread: const obj3 = {...obj1, ...obj2} (better).

  • modern es6 ka trika h same kaam krta h but cleaner  key conflict ko ignore krta h last wale ko maan ek chlta h 

Getting keys, values, entries

js
Object.keys(tinderUser);   // ["id","name","isLoggedIn"]
Object.values(tinderUser); // ["12ac","sammy",false]
Object.entries(tinderUser); // [["id","12ac"],["name","sammy"],["isLoggedIn",false]]

Checking property existence

js
tinderUser.hasOwnProperty("isLoggedIn"); // true

Object Destructuring

js
const course = {
  coursename: "js in hindi",
  price: 999,
  master: "hitesh"
};

phle hum like master ko pdkna hota tha toh hum aise krta the 
const master = course.master ; 
ab aise krte h 
kinda global krdiya 
const { master } = course;           // now master variable holds "hitesh"
but chatch here is hume master hi rkhan hota tha isme 
const { master: exe } = course;      // rename

and isme kya h ki humen master ka naam exe krdiya

Array Destructuring

js
const myArray = ["apple", "banana", "mango"];
const [first, second] = myArray;


internally its wokr like this 
const first = array[0]
const second = array[1]



const [first, , third] = myArray;
here third ko mango mil rha hoga 

const [a, b, c = "grapes"] = ["apple", "banana"];
default values bhi dekhst eh 

jese console.lgo(c) here is grapes

Functions

Function Declaration vs Expression

  • Declaration can be called before definition (hoisting).

  • hoisting sirf funtion add(a,b) me hoti h but if add = function(a,b) kiya isme hoisting nhi hoti ; isme toh baadme hi chalana pdeg a

  • Expression (assigning to variable) cannot be called before assignment.

🔥 Arrow Function (modern)

const add = (a, b) => a + b;

👉 Short syntax + widely used (React etc.)

iife() 

(function() {
  console.log("run immediately");
})();

👉 Immediately run hota hai

Parameters & Arguments

js
function addtwonumber(num1, num2) {
  return num1 + num2;
}
addtwonumber(3, "a"); // "3a" (string concatenation)

Return – default undefined if no return

js
function loginusermessage(username) {
  if (!username) {
    console.log("please enter a username");
    return;
  }
  return `${username} just logged in`;
}

Default parameters

js
function loginusermessage(username = "sam") { ... }

Rest operator in functions

js
function calculateprice(...num1) {
  return num1;   // array of all arguments
}

Passing objects to functions

js
function handleobject(anyobject) {
  console.log(`username is ${anyobject.username} and price is ${anyobject.price}`);
}
handleobject(user);

Scope

Block Scope (let, const vs var)

  • let and const are block‑scoped (inside {}).

  • var is function‑scoped, leaks out of block → avoid var.

Global scope

  • Variables declared outside any function are global.

  • Inside a block, variables can shadow outer variables.

this Keyword

In object methods

js
const user = {
  username: "hitesh",
  price: 999,
  welcomeMessage: function() {
    console.log(`${this.username} welcome to website`);
  }
};
user.welcomeMessage(); // "hitesh welcome to website"
user.username = 'meet';
user.welcomeMessage(); // "meet welcome to website"

  • this refers to the current object context.

In regular functions (global scope)

js
function username(){
  let username = 'meet';
  console.log(this.username); // undefined
}

Arrow functions – no own this

js
const chai = () => {
  let username = 'hitesh';
  console.log(this.username); // undefined
}

  • Arrow functions take this from surrounding (lexical) scope.

Arrow function syntax

js
// Explicit return
const addTwo = (n1, n2) => {
  return n1 + n2;
};
// Implicit return
const addTwo = (n1, n2) => n1 + n2;
yha pe automatically return krega but jb bhi object aayega toh bracket lgadena 
// Returning object (needs parentheses)
const addTwo = (num1, num2) => ({ username: 'hitesh' });
bina bracket ke undefined return krega

Immediately Invoked Function Expression (IIFE)

What is IIFE

  • Function that is executed immediately after definition.

  • Used to avoid global scope pollution.

Syntax

js
(function chai() {
  console.log("db connected");
})();   // named IIFE

Arrow function IIFE

js
(() => {
  console.log("db connected");
})();

Semicolon importance

  • IIFE should be preceded by a semicolon to avoid conflict with previous code.

Passing parameters

js
((name) => {
  console.log(`${name}`);
})("meet");

Named vs Anonymous IIFE

  • Named: (function meet() { ... })();

  • Anonymous: (() => { ... })();

1. Control Flow – If‑Else, Switch, Truthy/Falsy

if‑else

js
if (condition) {
  // runs if true
} else {
  // runs if false
}

Comparison operators: ==, ===, !=, !==, >, <, >=, <=

Short‑hand if (without braces):

js
if (balance > 500) console.log("test");

Nested if‑else

js
if (balance < 500) {
  console.log("less than 500");
} else if (balance < 750) {
  console.log("less than 750");
} else {
  console.log("less than 1200");
}

Logical operators:

  • && → all conditions must be true

  • || → at least one condition true

Switch statement:

js
switch (key) {
  case value1:
    // code
    break;  // without break, fall‑through happens
  case value2:
    // code
    break;
  default:
    // code
}

  • break stops further case execution.

  • If no break, all subsequent cases execute (fall‑through).

Example with string:

js
const month = "march";
switch (month) {
  case "jan": console.log("jan"); break;
  case "feb": console.log("feb"); break;
  case "march": console.log("march"); break;
  default: console.log("no match");
}
// Output: march

2. Truthy and Falsy Values

Falsy values (evaluated as false in conditions):

  • false

  • 0, -0, 0n (BigInt zero)

  • "" (empty string)

  • null

  • undefined

  • NaN

Truthy values (everything else):

  • "0", "false", " " (space)

  • [] (empty array)

  • {} (empty object)

  • function(){}

Checking empty array:

js
if (userEmail.length === 0) {
  console.log("array is empty");
}

Checking empty object:

js
if (Object.keys(emptyObj).length === 0) {
  console.log("object is empty");
}

Loose equality surprises:

js
false == 0   // true
false == ''  // true
0 == ''      // true

3. Nullish Coalescing Operator (??)

  • Checks only null and undefined, not other falsy values.

js
agar ?? se phle wala glt h toh second wala chlega nhi toh first wala 
hi chl rha hoga 

let val1 = 5 ?? 10;         // 5 (5 is not null/undefined)
let val2 = null ?? 10;      // 10
let val3 = undefined ?? 15; // 15
let val4 = null ?? 10 ?? 20; // 10 (first non‑null/undefined)

Difference between ?? and ||:

  • || checks any falsy value.  ( false, 0, "", null, undefined, NaN)

  • ?? checks only null/undefined.

js
console.log(0 || 100);   // 100 (0 is falsy)
console.log(0 ?? 100);   // 0   (0 is not null/undefined)

4. Ternary Operator

js
condition ? exprIfTrue : exprIfFalse

Example:

js
const meet = 100;
meet > 80 ? console.log("less than 80") : console.log("more than 80");
// Output: less than 80 (since 100 > 80 is true)

5. Loops

for loop

js
for (let i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
  console.log(i);
}

  • Initialization → condition check → code execution → increment, repeat.

while loop

js
let index = 5;
while (index > 0) {
  console.log(`value is ${index}`);
  index--;
}

do‑while loop

js
let num = 0;
do {
  console.log(num);
  num++;
} while (num < 3);

  • Runs at least once, then checks condition.

break and continue

  • break → exits loop completely.

  • continue → skips current iteration, moves to next.

js
for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
  if (i === 3) break;   // stops at 3
  console.log(i);       // 0,1,2
}

for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
  if (i === 3) continue; // skip 3
  console.log(i);        // 0,1,2,4
}

6. High Order Array Loops

for...of loop

  • Works on iterables (arrays, strings, maps, sets).

  • Gives values directly.

js
const arr = [1, 2, 3];
for (const num of arr) {
  console.log(num);   // 1,2,3
}

for...in loop

  • Works on enumerable properties (keys/indexes).

  • Use on objects, arrays (gives indexes).

js
const myobject = {
  js: "JavaScript",
  cpp: "C++",
  rb: "Ruby"
};
for (const key in myobject) {
  console.log(`${key} shortcut is for ${myobject[key]}`);
}
// Output: js shortcut is for JavaScript ...

For arrays, for...in gives indexes:

js
const programming = ["js", "rb", "py", "java"];
for (const key in programming) {
  console.log(key);   // 0,1,2,3
}

forEach loop (higher order function)

  • Callback executed for each element.

  • Does not return anything (always undefined).

js
const coding = ["js", "ruby", "java", "python", "cpp"];

// with anonymous function
coding.forEach(function (val) {
  console.log(val);
});

// with arrow function
coding.forEach((item) => {
  console.log(item);
});

// passing function reference
function printMe(item) {
  console.log(item);
}
coding.forEach(printMe);

// with index and array
coding.forEach((item, index, arr) => {
  console.log(item, index, arr);
});

forEach with array of objects:

js
const mycoding = [
  { languagename: "javascript", languagefilename: "js" },
  { languagename: "python", languagefilename: "py" }
];
mycoding.forEach((item) => {
  console.log(item);
  console.log(item.languagename);
});

forEach does not return values – always undefined.

7. map

  • Creates a new array by applying a function to each element.

  • Returns the new array.

js
const mynum = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6];
const newnums = mynum.map((num) => num + 10);
console.log(newnums); // [11,12,13,14,15,16]

Explicit return with {}

js
const newnums = mynum.map((num) => {
  return num + 10;
});

Map chaining:

js
const newNums = myNum
  .map((num) => num * 10)   // [10,20,30,40,50,60]
  .map((num) => num + 1)    // [11,21,31,41,51,61]
  .filter((num) => num > 40); // [41,51,61]

8. filter

  • Creates a new array with elements that satisfy a condition.

js
const mynums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6];
const newnums = mynums.filter((num) => num > 4);
console.log(newnums); // [5,6]

If using {} (explicit return needed):

js
const newnums = mynums.filter((num) => {
  return num > 4;
}); // [5,6]

Without return (incorrect):

js
const newnums = mynums.filter((num) => {
  num > 4;
}); // []

Using forEach to simulate filter (manual):

js
const newnums = [];
mynums.forEach((num) => {
  if (num > 4) {
    newnums.push(num);
  }
});

Filtering array of objects:

js
const books = [
  { title: "Book One", genre: "History", publish: 1990 },
  { title: "Book Two", genre: "Science", publish: 2005 },
  { title: "Book Three", genre: "History", publish: 2005 }
];
const historyBooks = books.filter((bk) => bk.genre === "History");
const recentBooks = books.filter((bk) => {
  return bk.publish >= 2000;
});

9. reduce

  • Reduces array to a single value (e.g., sum, product).

  • Takes callback (accumulator, currentValue) and initial value.

js
const array1 = [1, 2, 3, 4];
const result = array1.reduce((accumulator, currentValue) => {
  return accumulator + currentValue;
}, 0);
console.log(result); // 10

Step‑by‑step:

  • Initial accumulator = 0, current = 1 → returns 1

  • accumulator = 1, current = 2 → returns 3

  • accumulator = 3, current = 3 → returns 6

  • accumulator = 6, current = 4 → returns 10

Logging inside reduce:

js
const x = mynums.reduce((acc, currval) => {
  console.log(`acc: ${acc} and currval: ${currval}`);
  return acc + currval;
}, 0);

Shopping cart example:

js
const shoppingcart = [
  { itemname: "js", price: 2999 },
  { itemname: "web", price: 999 }
];
const total = shoppingcart.reduce((acc, item) => acc + item.price, 0);
console.log(total); // 3998

10. Summary of Array Methods

MethodPurposeReturns
forEachExecute function on each elementundefined
mapTransform each element, create new arraynew array
filterKeep elements that satisfy conditionnew array
reduceCombine elements into a single valueaccumulated value

Chainability: map().map().filter() works because each returns a new array.
